title: Best Time to Buy & Sell Stock I (DP view).
description: Best Time to Buy & Sell Stock I (DP view).

  j=2: profit = 1-3 = -2, max_profit=0

i=1 (buy price=2):

All profits are negative or zero, so max_profit remains 0

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Check every possible pair of buy and sell days by iterating through the array with nested loops to find the maximum profit. Use this for understanding the problem but avoid for large inputs due to inefficiency.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Maximum Profit Finder
ladders.desc: Complete nested loop solution checking all buy-sell pairs to find maximum profit.
ladders.explanation:
This is the complete brute force solution to the Best Time to Buy & Sell Stock I problem. The intuition is straightforward: since we can only buy once and sell once, we need to consider every possible pair of days where we buy on day i and sell on day j (with j > i). For each such pair, we calculate the profit (prices[j] - prices[i]) and track the maximum profit found. This approach works because it exhaustively checks all possibilities, guaranteeing we find the optimal solution. However, it's inefficient for large inputs due to its O(n²) time complexity.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize max_profit = 0
2. For i from 0 to n-2 (buy day):
   a. For j from i+1 to n-1 (sell day):
      i. Calculate profit = prices[j] - prices[i]
      ii. If profit > max_profit, update max_profit = profit
3. Return max_profit

Time complexity: O(n²) - We have nested loops where the inner loop runs approximately n times for each i, leading to roughly n*(n-1)/2 operations.
Space complexity: O(1) - We only use a few variables regardless of input size.

Connection to L1: This full solution builds upon the key subroutine of comparing two prices to calculate profit, applying it systematically across all possible pairs.

Common mistakes:
1. Starting j from i instead of i+1 (would allow selling on same day as buying)
2. Not handling empty or single-element arrays (should return 0 profit)
3. Forgetting to initialize max_profit to 0 (could return undefined)
4. Using <= instead of < in loop conditions (could cause index out of bounds)

Real code pattern:
def max_profit_brute_force(prices):
    n = len(prices)
    max_profit = 0
    for i in range(n-1):
        for j in range(i+1, n):
            profit = prices[j] - prices[i]
            if profit > max_profit:
                max_profit = profit
    return max_profit

examples.trace:

  j=3: profit = 3-1 = 2, max_profit=4
  j=4: profit = 6-1 = 5, max_profit=5
  j=5: profit = 4-1 = 3, max_profit=5

i=2 (buy price=5):
  j=3: profit = 3-5 = -2, max_profit=5
  j=4: profit = 6-5 = 1, max_profit=5
  j=5: profit = 4-5 = -1, max_profit=5

i=3 (buy price=3):
  j=4: profit = 6-3 = 3, max_profit=5
  j=5: profit = 4-3 = 1, max_profit=5

i=4 (buy price=6):
  j=5: profit = 4-6 = -2, max_profit=5

Final max_profit = 5

Array: [3,2,1]
Initialize max_profit = 0

i=0 (buy price=3):
  j=1: profit = 2-3 = -1, max_profit=0
  j=2: profit = 1-3 = -2, max_profit=0

i=1 (buy price=2):

All profits are negative or zero, so max_profit remains 0

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Pairwise Profit Calculator
ladders.desc: Calculate profit for all sell days given a fixed buy day.
ladders.explanation:
This is the key subroutine in the brute force approach. Given a fixed buy day index i, we need to calculate profits for all possible sell days j where j > i. The intuition is that once we've chosen a buy day, we need to explore all future days as potential sell days to find the maximum profit achievable from that particular buy day. This subroutine captures the essence of the problem: for each buying opportunity, what's the best selling opportunity that follows?

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Input: prices array, buy_day index i
2. Initialize best_profit_from_i = 0
3. For j from i+1 to n-1:
   a. Calculate profit = prices[j] - prices[i]
   b. If profit > best_profit_from_i, update best_profit_from_i = profit
4. Return best_profit_from_i

Time complexity: O(n) in worst case when i=0, but average O(n) for each i.
Space complexity: O(1) - constant extra space.

Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each possible buy day i in the full solution. The full solution essentially runs this subroutine n-1 times (for i from 0 to n-2) and tracks the maximum result.

Connection to L2: This builds upon the core logic of comparing two prices, applying it repeatedly for a fixed first price.

Common mistakes:
1. Starting j from i instead of i+1 (would calculate profit of 0 for same day)
2. Not resetting best_profit_from_i for each new i in the full solution
3. Using absolute value instead of actual difference

Real code pattern:
def max_profit_from_buy_day(prices, buy_day):
    best_profit = 0
    buy_price = prices[buy_day]
    for sell_day in range(buy_day+1, len(prices)):
        profit = prices[sell_day] - buy_price
        if profit > best_profit:
            best_profit = profit
    return best_profit

examples.trace:
Array: [7,1,5,3,6,4], buy_day=1 (buy price=1)
Initialize best_profit = 0

j=2: profit = 5-1 = 4, best_profit=4
j=3: profit = 3-1 = 2, best_profit=4
j=4: profit = 6-1 = 5, best_profit=5
j=5: profit = 4-1 = 3, best_profit=5

Final best_profit = 5

Array: [3,2,1], buy_day=0 (buy price=3)
Initialize best_profit = 0

j=1: profit = 2-3 = -1, best_profit=0
j=2: profit = 1-3 = -2, best_profit=0

All profits negative, best_profit remains 0
Final best_profit = 0

ladders.title: Core Logic — Price Difference Comparison
ladders.desc: Compare two prices to determine if selling later yields profit.
ladders.explanation:
This is the core logic at the heart of the stock trading problem: comparing two prices to determine if a profit can be made by buying at the earlier price and selling at the later price. The intuition is simple - profit occurs when the sell price is higher than the buy price. This fundamental comparison is what drives the entire algorithm. Every trading decision boils down to this basic calculation: sell_price - buy_price.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Input: buy_price, sell_price
2. Calculate difference = sell_price - buy_price
3. If difference > 0, it represents profit
4. If difference <= 0, it represents loss or break-even

Time complexity: O(1) - constant time operation.
Space complexity: O(1) - uses only a few variables.

Connection to L1: This comparison is performed repeatedly in the pairwise profit calculator for each potential sell day j given a fixed buy day i.

Connection to L3: This builds upon the building block of subtraction, applying it to the specific context of price comparison.

Common mistakes:
1. Subtracting in wrong order (buy_price - sell_price instead of sell_price - buy_price)
2. Comparing absolute values instead of signed differences
3. Not considering zero difference as valid (break-even is allowed)

Real code pattern:
def calculate_profit(buy_price, sell_price):
    return sell_price - buy_price

# Usage:
profit = calculate_profit(buy_price, sell_price)
if profit > max_profit:
    max_profit = profit

examples.trace:
buy_price = 5, sell_price = 8
Calculate: sell_price - buy_price = 8 - 5 = 3
Since 3 > 0, this represents a profit of 3

buy_price = 10, sell_price = 7
Calculate: sell_price - buy_price = 7 - 10 = -3
Since -3 < 0, this represents a loss of 3 (or negative profit)

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Traversal with Nested Loops
ladders.desc: Use nested loops to compare each element with all subsequent elements.
ladders.explanation:
This building block demonstrates the pattern of using nested loops to compare each element in an array with all elements that come after it. The intuition is that we need to consider all possible ordered pairs (i, j) where i < j. This pattern appears in many brute force algorithms where we need to examine relationships between all pairs of elements. In the stock problem context, i represents the buy day and j represents the sell day, and we must have i < j since we must buy before we can sell.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Input: array arr of length n
2. For i from 0 to n-2:
   a. For j from i+1 to n-1:
      i. Process pair (arr[i], arr[j])

Time complexity: O(n²) - the inner loop runs approximately n times for each i.
Space complexity: O(1) - only loop counters needed.

Connection to L2: This loop structure provides the framework within which we apply the price difference comparison to all relevant pairs.

Connection to L4: This builds upon the basic operation of array indexing, using it in a structured nested pattern.

Common mistakes:
1. Starting j from 0 instead of i+1 (would compare each pair twice and include i=j cases)
2. Using i <= n-1 instead of i <= n-2 (would cause j loop to start at n when i=n-1)
3. Not understanding that j must always be greater than i

Real code pattern:
n = len(arr)
for i in range(n-1):
    for j in range(i+1, n):
        # Process arr[i] and arr[j]
        # arr[i] comes before arr[j] in the array

examples.trace:
Array: [7,1,5,3], n=4

i=0 (element=7):
  j=1: pair (7,1)
  j=2: pair (7,5)
  j=3: pair (7,3)

i=1 (element=1):
  j=2: pair (1,5)
  j=3: pair (1,3)

i=2 (element=5):
  j=3: pair (5,3)

Total pairs: 6
Note: j always starts at i+1, so we get ordered pairs where first element comes before second

Array: [2,4,6], n=3

i=0 (element=2):
  j=1: pair (2,4)
  j=2: pair (2,6)

i=1 (element=4):
  j=2: pair (4,6)

Total pairs: 3
Each pair has i < j

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Indexing and Element Access
ladders.desc: Access elements in an array using indices and understand array bounds.
ladders.explanation:
This basic operation involves accessing individual elements in an array using their indices. The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and we can retrieve any element in constant time if we know its index. Understanding zero-based indexing (where the first element is at index 0) and array bounds (valid indices range from 0 to length-1) is crucial for working with arrays in programming. In the stock problem, we need to access prices[i] for the buy price and prices[j] for the sell price.

Key concepts:
1. Zero-based indexing: First element at index 0, last at index n-1
2. Array length: Number of elements, accessible as len(arr) in Python
3. Bounds checking: Accessing outside 0 to n-1 causes errors
4. Element retrieval: arr[i] gets the element at position i

Time complexity: O(1) for accessing any element by index.
Space complexity: O(1) for the access operation itself.

Connection to L3: Array indexing is used within the nested loops to retrieve the specific prices we want to compare.

Connection to L5: This builds upon the foundation concept of arrays as ordered collections.

Common mistakes:
1. Using 1-based indexing (thinking arr[1] is first element)
2. Accessing arr[n] instead of arr[n-1] for last element
3. Not checking if index is within bounds before accessing
4. Confusing array length with last index

Real code pattern:
# Accessing elements
first_element = arr[0]
last_element = arr[len(arr)-1]

# Iterating with indices
for i in range(len(arr)):
    current_element = arr[i]
    # process current_element

examples.trace:
Array: [10,20,30,40]
Indices: 0:10, 1:20, 2:30, 3:40
Length: 4 (valid indices: 0,1,2,3)
Access arr[2]:
- Start at index 0 (value 10)
- Move to index 1 (value 20)
- Move to index 2 (value 30)
Result: 30

Array: [5,15,25]
Indices: 0:5, 1:15, 2:25
Length: 3 (valid indices: 0,1,2)
Access arr[0]:
- First element is at index 0
- Value at index 0 is 5
Result: 5

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays as Ordered Collections
ladders.desc: Understand arrays as ordered sequences of elements accessible by position.
ladders.explanation:
This is the foundational concept: understanding what an array is and how it works. An array is a data structure that stores a collection of elements in a specific order. The intuition is simple - think of it like a row of boxes numbered starting from 0, where each box can hold one value. The order matters: the element at position 0 comes first, then position 1, and so on. This ordered nature is crucial for the stock problem because stock prices are given in chronological order - prices[0] is day 0's price, prices[1] is day 1's price, etc. We can only buy before selling, which corresponds to accessing earlier elements (lower indices) as buy prices and later elements (higher indices) as sell prices.

Key characteristics:
1. Ordered: Elements have positions (indices) starting from 0
2. Fixed size: Typically has a defined length when created
3. Homogeneous: Usually contains elements of the same type
4. Random access: Can access any element directly if you know its position

In the stock problem context:
- The array represents daily stock prices over time
- Index represents day number
- prices[i] is the price on day i
- We must have i < j for buying on day i and selling on day j

Connection to L4: This foundation enables the basic operation of accessing elements by their indices.

Common beginner misunderstandings:
1. Thinking arrays start at index 1
2. Not understanding that order corresponds to time sequence
3. Confusing array values with array indices
4. Not realizing that array length gives count of elements, not last index

Real code pattern:
# Creating an array (list in Python)
prices = [7, 1, 5, 3, 6, 4]

# Understanding the structure:
# Index:   0  1  2  3  4  5
# Value:   7  1  5  3  6  4
# Day:     0  1  2  3  4  5

# Day 0 price: prices[0] = 7
# Day 1 price: prices[1] = 1
# etc.

examples.trace:
Array: [100, 150, 120, 180]
This represents 4 consecutive days of stock prices:
- Index 0 = Day 0 price = 100
- Index 1 = Day 1 price = 150
- Index 2 = Day 2 price = 120
- Index 3 = Day 3 price = 180

Length = 4 (4 days of data)
Valid indices: 0, 1, 2, 3

If we buy on day 1 (index 1, price 150) and sell on day 3 (index 3, price 180):
Profit = prices[3] - prices[1] = 180 - 150 = 30

examples.input: Array: [5, 5, 5, 5] representing 4 days of constant price
examples.trace:
Array: [5, 5, 5, 5]
This represents 4 days where the stock price never changes:
- Day 0: price = 5 (index 0)
- Day 1: price = 5 (index 1)
- Day 2: price = 5 (index 2)
- Day 3: price = 5 (index 3)

Length = 4
All elements have same value

If we buy on any day and sell on any later day:
Profit = sell_price - buy_price = 5 - 5 = 0
No profit possible since price never changes

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with Array O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a DP array to track the minimum price seen so far and compute maximum profit by iterating once through the prices. This leverages DP to store intermediate results for efficiency.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Best Time to Buy & Sell Stock I with DP Array
ladders.desc: Complete solution using DP array to track minimum price and compute maximum profit in O(n) time.
ladders.explanation: This problem asks: given an array of daily stock prices, find the maximum profit possible by buying on one day and selling on a later day. The DP approach maintains a dp array where dp[i] stores the minimum price seen up to day i. For each day i, we compute potential profit as prices[i] - dp[i-1] (current price minus minimum price before today). The maximum profit across all days is the answer. Intuition: The optimal sell day must come after the optimal buy day, so tracking the minimum price before each day allows us to compute the best profit if we sell on that day. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize dp array of same length as prices, with dp[0] = prices[0] (minimum so far is first price). 2. Initialize maxProfit = 0. 3. For i from 1 to n-1: a. dp[i] = min(dp[i-1], prices[i]) (update minimum price). b. profit = prices[i] - dp[i-1] (profit if selling today). c. maxProfit = max(maxProfit, profit). 4. Return maxProfit. Time: O(n) for single pass. Space: O(n) for dp array. Connection to L1: This uses the key subroutine of tracking minimum price in dp array. Common mistakes: Using dp[i] instead of dp[i-1] for profit calculation (dp[i] includes today's price, but we need minimum before selling), or initializing maxProfit incorrectly for negative scenarios. Real code pattern: def maxProfit(prices): n = len(prices); if n < 2: return 0; dp = [0]*n; dp[0] = prices[0]; max_profit = 0; for i in range(1, n): dp[i] = min(dp[i-1], prices[i]); profit = prices[i] - dp[i-1]; max_profit = max(max_profit, profit); return max_profit.

examples.trace: prices = [7,1,5,3,6,4], n=6. Initialize dp[0]=7, maxProfit=0. i=1: dp[1]=min(dp[0]=7, prices[1]=1)=1, profit=prices[1]-dp[0]=1-7=-6, maxProfit=max(0,-6)=0. i=2: dp[2]=min(dp[1]=1,5)=1, profit=5-1=4, maxProfit=max(0,4)=4. i=3: dp[3]=min(1,3)=1, profit=3-1=2, maxProfit=4. i=4: dp[4]=min(1,6)=1, profit=6-1=5, maxProfit=5. i=5: dp[5]=min(1,4)=1, profit=4-1=3, maxProfit=5. Return 5.

prices = [7,6,4,3,1], n=5. dp[0]=7, maxProfit=0. i=1: dp[1]=min(7,6)=6, profit=6-7=-1, maxProfit=0. i=2: dp[2]=min(6,4)=4, profit=4-6=-2, maxProfit=0. i=3: dp[3]=min(4,3)=3, profit=3-4=-1, maxProfit=0. i=4: dp[4]=min(3,1)=1, profit=1-3=-2, maxProfit=0. Return 0.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Tracking Minimum Price with DP Array
ladders.desc: Build a DP array that stores the minimum price seen up to each index.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine creates a dp array where dp[i] = minimum price from prices[0] to prices[i]. It's the core data structure for the full solution. Intuition: To compute profit when selling on day i, we need the lowest price before i (buy day). By storing cumulative minimums, we avoid re-scanning the array. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize dp array of length n (n = len(prices)). 2. Set dp[0] = prices[0] (first element is trivially the minimum so far). 3. For i from 1 to n-1: dp[i] = min(dp[i-1], prices[i]). This compares the previous minimum with current price. Time: O(n) for one pass. Space: O(n) for dp array. Connection to L0: This dp array is used in L0 to compute profits. Connection to L2: This implements the core logic of maintaining state. Common mistakes: Forgetting to initialize dp[0], or using prices[i] directly without comparison. Real code pattern: def minPriceArray(prices): n = len(prices); dp = [0]*n; dp[0] = prices[0]; for i in range(1, n): dp[i] = min(dp[i-1], prices[i]); return dp.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Compute Profit Using Previous Minimum
ladders.desc: Calculate potential profit for each day by subtracting the minimum price before it.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique: for each day i (as potential sell day), profit = prices[i] - minPriceBefore[i], where minPriceBefore[i] is the minimum price from days 0 to i-1. Intuition: The best profit if selling on day i comes from buying at the lowest price before i. By iterating through days, we compute this for all possible sell days and track the maximum. Step-by-step: Given prices array and minBefore array (from L1), initialize maxProfit = 0. For i from 1 to n-1: profit = prices[i] - minBefore[i-1]; maxProfit = max(maxProfit, profit). Note: Start at i=1 because you need at least one day before to buy. Time: O(n) for the loop. Space: O(1) extra if minBefore is given. Connection to L1: Uses minBefore array from L1. Connection to L3: Relies on array indexing and comparison operations. Common mistakes: Using minBefore[i] instead of minBefore[i-1] (which would allow buying and selling same day, invalid), or not handling empty arrays. Real code pattern: def computeProfit(prices, minBefore): max_profit = 0; for i in range(1, len(prices)): profit = prices[i] - minBefore[i-1]; max_profit = max(max_profit, profit); return max_profit.

examples.trace: prices = [7,1,5,3,6,4], minBefore = [7,1,1,1,1,1], n=6. maxProfit=0. i=1: profit=prices[1]-minBefore[0]=1-7=-6, maxProfit=0. i=2: profit=5-1=4, maxProfit=4. i=3: profit=3-1=2, maxProfit=4. i=4: profit=6-1=5, maxProfit=5. i=5: profit=4-1=3, maxProfit=5. Return 5.

prices = [3,5,2,8,1], minBefore = [3,3,2,2,1], n=5. maxProfit=0. i=1: profit=5-3=2, maxProfit=2. i=2: profit=2-3=-1, maxProfit=2. i=3: profit=8-2=6, maxProfit=6. i=4: profit=1-2=-1, maxProfit=6. Return 6.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterate Array and Update Maximum
ladders.desc: Loop through an array, compute values, and keep track of the maximum.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves traversing an array, performing calculations at each step, and maintaining a running maximum. Intuition: Many algorithms require scanning data once while accumulating results (like max profit). Step-by-step: 1. Initialize a variable (e.g., max_val) to an appropriate starting value (often 0 or first element). 2. For each element in array (or from a specific index): a. Compute some value based on current element and possibly previous data. b. Update max_val = max(max_val, computed_value). 3. Return max_val. Time: O(n) for the loop. Space: O(1) for variables. Connection to L2: This pattern is used in L2 to track max profit. Connection to L4: Uses comparison and assignment operations. Common mistakes: Incorrect initialization (e.g., using 0 when negative values possible), or not updating correctly. Real code pattern: def findMax(arr): max_val = arr[0] if len(arr)>0 else 0; for val in arr: # Or compute something from val current = val * 2; # Example computation max_val = max(max_val, current); return max_val.

examples.trace: arr = [3,1,4,2]. Initialize max_val = 3*2=6 (first element doubled). i=1: current=1*2=2, max_val=max(6,2)=6. i=2: current=4*2=8, max_val=max(6,8)=8. i=3: current=2*2=4, max_val=8. Return 8.

examples.input: Compute max of (element - previous) for [5,3,8,6], starting at index 1
examples.trace: arr = [5,3,8,6]. Initialize max_val = arr[1]-arr[0]=3-5=-2. i=2: current=8-3=5, max_val=max(-2,5)=5. i=3: current=6-8=-2, max_val=5. Return 5.

testCases.input: Max of cumulative sum up to each index for [1,2,3,4]
Max of (element - min_so_far) with min_so_far=[3,3,2,2,1] for [3,5,2,8,1]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare and Assign Minimum/Maximum
ladders.desc: Use min() and max() functions or manual comparisons to update variables.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves comparing two values and assigning the smaller (for minimum) or larger (for maximum) to a variable. Intuition: Tracking extremes (like min price or max profit) requires updating variables based on comparisons. Step-by-step: For minimum: current_min = min(current_min, new_value). For maximum: current_max = max(current_max, new_value). Alternatively, use if statements: if new_value < current_min: current_min = new_value. Time: O(1) per operation. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This is used in L3 to update max_val. Connection to L5: Builds on comparison operations. Common mistakes: Forgetting to initialize variables, or using wrong comparison signs. Real code pattern: a = 5; b = 3; min_val = min(a, b); # min_val becomes 3. Or: max_val = 0; new = 4; if new > max_val: max_val = new.

examples.input: Update min: start with min_val=7, new values [1,5,3]
examples.trace: Initialize min_val=7. Process new=1: min_val = min(7,1)=1. Process new=5: min_val = min(1,5)=1. Process new=3: min_val = min(1,3)=1. Final min_val=1.

examples.input: Update max: start with max_val=0, new values [-2,4,1]
examples.trace: Initialize max_val=0. Process new=-2: max_val = max(0,-2)=0. Process new=4: max_val = max(0,4)=4. Process new=1: max_val = max(4,1)=4. Final max_val=4.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Traversal with Index
ladders.desc: Loop through an array using indices to access elements sequentially.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: iterating over an array from start to end using an index variable. Intuition: Algorithms often process each element in order, and indices allow access to current and previous elements. Step-by-step: For an array arr of length n, use a loop: for i in range(n): access arr[i]. To start from a specific index, e.g., for i in range(1, n) starts at index 1. Time: O(n) for traversing n elements. Space: O(1) for index variable. Connection to L4: This traversal enables comparisons at each step. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors (e.g., using i <= n), or not handling empty arrays. Real code pattern: arr = [7,1,5]; for i in range(len(arr)): print(arr[i]). Or: for i in range(1, len(arr)): print(arr[i] - arr[i-1]).

examples.trace: arr = [7,1,5], n=3. i=0: print arr[0]=7. i=1: print arr[1]=1. i=2: print arr[2]=5. Output: 7,1,5.

examples.input: Traverse [3,5,2] from index 1 and print difference from previous
examples.trace: arr = [3,5,2], n=3. Loop i from 1 to 2. i=1: print arr[1]-arr[0]=5-3=2. i=2: print arr[2]-arr[1]=2-5=-3. Output: 2,-3.

testCases.input: Traverse [4,2] from start and compute sum
Traverse [7,1,5] and access arr[i-1] for i=1,2

approaches.name: Greedy Single Pass O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through the prices once, keeping track of the minimum price encountered and updating the maximum profit greedily. This is optimal and space-efficient, treating it as a greedy or DP problem without extra storage.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Best Time to Buy & Sell Stock I with Greedy Single Pass
ladders.desc: Complete solution finding maximum profit by buying low and selling high using single iteration.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the maximum profit achievable by buying on one day and selling on a later day. The greedy single pass approach solves this optimally in O(n) time and O(1) space by maintaining two key variables: the minimum price seen so far (min_price) and the maximum profit achievable (max_profit). The intuition is that for each price, we consider it as a potential selling price. The best buying price for that selling day would be the minimum price encountered before that day. By tracking the minimum price as we iterate, we can compute the profit for each day as price - min_price and update max_profit if this profit is higher. This works because profit = selling_price - buying_price, and for any selling day, the optimal buying day is the day with the minimum price before it. The algorithm initializes min_price to infinity (or a very large number) and max_profit to 0. Then, for each price in the array: 1) Update min_price = min(min_price, current_price). 2) Calculate current_profit = current_price - min_price. 3) Update max_profit = max(max_profit, current_profit). After iterating through all prices, max_profit contains the answer. Time complexity is O(n) as we traverse the array once. Space complexity is O(1) since we use only two variables. This connects to the parent problem by implementing the complete greedy logic. Common mistakes include initializing min_price incorrectly (e.g., to 0 instead of a large value), not handling empty arrays (return 0), or trying to compute profit before updating min_price (which would give negative profit). Real code pattern: def maxProfit(prices): min_price = float('inf'); max_profit = 0; for price in prices: min_price = min(min_price, price); profit = price - min_price; max_profit = max(max_profit, profit); return max_profit.

examples.trace: Initialize min_price = inf, max_profit = 0. Price 7: min_price = min(inf, 7) = 7, profit = 7-7 = 0, max_profit = max(0,0) = 0. Price 1: min_price = min(7,1) = 1, profit = 1-1 = 0, max_profit = max(0,0) = 0. Price 5: min_price = min(1,5) = 1, profit = 5-1 = 4, max_profit = max(0,4) = 4. Price 3: min_price = min(1,3) = 1, profit = 3-1 = 2, max_profit = max(4,2) = 4. Price 6: min_price = min(1,6) = 1, profit = 6-1 = 5, max_profit = max(4,5) = 5. Price 4: min_price = min(1,4) = 1, profit = 4-1 = 3, max_profit = max(5,3) = 5. Return 5.

Initialize min_price = inf, max_profit = 0. Price 7: min_price = min(inf,7) = 7, profit = 7-7 = 0, max_profit = 0. Price 6: min_price = min(7,6) = 6, profit = 6-6 = 0, max_profit = 0. Price 4: min_price = min(6,4) = 4, profit = 4-4 = 0, max_profit = 0. Price 3: min_price = min(4,3) = 3, profit = 3-3 = 0, max_profit = 0. Price 1: min_price = min(3,1) = 1, profit = 1-1 = 0, max_profit = 0. Return 0.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Tracking Minimum Price and Computing Daily Profit
ladders.desc: The core loop that updates minimum price and calculates profit for each day.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the iterative process at the heart of the greedy approach. For each price in the sequence, it performs two operations: update the minimum price seen so far, and compute the profit if selling at the current price. The intuition is that to maximize profit, we need to know the lowest buying price available before each potential selling day. By maintaining a running minimum, we can instantly compute the best possible profit for selling on any given day. The algorithm works as: Initialize min_price to a very high value (e.g., infinity). Then for each current_price: 1) Update min_price = minimum(min_price, current_price). This ensures min_price always holds the lowest price encountered up to this point. 2) Calculate profit = current_price - min_price. This represents the profit if we bought at the lowest price so far and sold at current_price. This sub-routine is executed for every element in the array. Time complexity per iteration is O(1), and overall O(n) for n prices. Space complexity is O(1) as only min_price is stored. This feeds into the parent problem (L0) by providing the mechanism to compute potential profits; the full problem then takes the maximum of these profits. Common mistakes include updating min_price after calculating profit (which would use current price as both buy and sell), or not initializing min_price properly. Real code pattern: min_price = float('inf'); for price in prices: min_price = min(min_price, price); profit = price - min_price; # Then use profit in L0.

examples.trace: Initialize min_price = inf. Price 5: min_price = min(inf,5) = 5, profit = 5-5 = 0. Price 3: min_price = min(5,3) = 3, profit = 3-3 = 0. Price 8: min_price = min(3,8) = 3, profit = 8-3 = 5. Price 1: min_price = min(3,1) = 1, profit = 1-1 = 0. Price 4: min_price = min(1,4) = 1, profit = 4-1 = 3. Output lists min prices and profits.

Initialize min_price = inf. Price 10: min_price = min(inf,10) = 10, profit = 10-10 = 0. Price 9: min_price = min(10,9) = 9, profit = 9-9 = 0. Price 8: min_price = min(9,8) = 8, profit = 8-8 = 0. Price 7: min_price = min(8,7) = 7, profit = 7-7 = 0. Price 6: min_price = min(7,6) = 6, profit = 6-6 = 0.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Greedy Update of Maximum Profit
ladders.desc: The technique of maintaining and updating the maximum profit encountered during iteration.
ladders.explanation: This core logic focuses on how to determine the overall maximum profit from the sequence of daily profits computed in L1. The intuition is greedy: as we calculate profit for each day (selling price - minimum price so far), we keep track of the highest profit seen so far. This works because we're considering every possible selling day, and for each, we pair it with the best buying day (minimum price before it). By taking the maximum across all these daily profits, we find the global maximum profit. The algorithm involves initializing max_profit to 0 (since profit cannot be negative in this problem—if no profit is possible, answer is 0). Then, for each profit calculated (from L1), update max_profit = maximum(max_profit, current_profit). This ensures max_profit always holds the highest value encountered. Time complexity is O(1) per update, O(n) overall. Space is O(1) for max_profit. This connects to L1 by using the profits computed there; together they form the complete greedy pass. Common mistakes include initializing max_profit to a negative value or not updating it correctly. Real code pattern: max_profit = 0; for profit in profits: max_profit = max(max_profit, profit); return max_profit.

examples.trace: Initialize max_profit = 0. Profit 0: max_profit = max(0,0) = 0. Profit 0: max_profit = max(0,0) = 0. Profit 5: max_profit = max(0,5) = 5. Profit 0: max_profit = max(5,0) = 5. Profit 3: max_profit = max(5,3) = 5. Return 5.

Initialize max_profit = 0. Profit 0: max_profit = max(0,0) = 0. Profit 0: max_profit = max(0,0) = 0. Profit 0: max_profit = max(0,0) = 0. Profit 0: max_profit = max(0,0) = 0. Profit 0: max_profit = max(0,0) = 0. Return 0.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Through an Array with Running Minimum
ladders.desc: The concept of traversing a list while keeping track of the minimum value seen so far.
ladders.explanation: This building block teaches how to iterate through an array and maintain a running minimum. The intuition is that in many problems, we need to know the smallest element encountered up to a certain point without re-scanning the array. This is done by initializing a variable (min_val) to a very large value (or the first element), then for each element in the array, update min_val = min(min_val, current_element). This ensures min_val always holds the minimum of all elements processed so far. This is efficient because it uses O(1) time per element and O(1) space. This connects to L2 and L1 by providing the mechanism to track the minimum price, which is essential for computing profits. Common mistakes include initializing min_val to 0 (which might be smaller than actual values) or not handling empty arrays. Real code pattern: min_val = float('inf'); for num in arr: min_val = min(min_val, num); # min_val now holds overall minimum.

examples.trace: Initialize min_val = inf. Element 5: min_val = min(inf,5) = 5. Element 2: min_val = min(5,2) = 2. Element 8: min_val = min(2,8) = 2. Element 1: min_val = min(2,1) = 1. Element 4: min_val = min(1,4) = 1. Output list of running minimums.
Initialize min_val = inf. Element 3: min_val = min(inf,3) = 3. Element 3: min_val = min(3,3) = 3. Element 3: min_val = min(3,3) = 3.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Computing Difference Between Two Numbers
ladders.desc: The fundamental arithmetic operation of subtracting one number from another to find profit.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves calculating the difference between two numbers: profit = selling_price - buying_price. In the context of the stock problem, this represents the profit earned if you buy at buying_price and sell at selling_price. The intuition is simple: profit is the gain (or loss if negative) from a transaction. This operation is performed for each day using the current price as selling_price and the minimum price so far as buying_price. It's a constant-time O(1) operation. This connects to L3 by using the running minimum as buying_price. Common mistakes include subtracting in wrong order (buying_price - selling_price gives negative profit) or not handling cases where selling_price is less than buying_price (profit is negative, but in this problem we ignore negatives since we can choose not to transact). Real code pattern: profit = selling_price - buying_price.

examples.trace: Profit = 10 - 4 = 6.
Profit = 3 - 7 = -4 (but in problem, we take max with 0).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Simple Iteration
ladders.desc: The prerequisite skill of working with lists and looping through elements.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the basics of arrays (or lists) and iteration, which are essential for the greedy approach. An array is a collection of elements stored in contiguous memory, accessible by index. Iteration means visiting each element of the array in sequence, typically using a loop. The intuition is that to process data stored in an array, we often need to examine each item one by one. For example, in Python, you can use a for loop: for price in prices: # do something with price. This allows performing operations on each element, such as comparing values or computing sums. Time complexity for iterating through an array of size n is O(n). Space complexity is O(1) if no extra data structures are used. This connects to L4 by providing the structure to access selling prices. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices or misunderstanding loop ranges. Real code pattern: prices = [7,1,5]; for price in prices: print(price) # prints 7, then 1, then 5.

examples.trace: Start loop: first element is 2, process it. Move to next: element is 4, process it. Move to next: element is 6, process it. End of array.
Start loop: only element is 10, process it. End of array.
